content:
# Genesis Chapter 9 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 5v–6r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_009_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_009_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 6–7 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** And God blessed Noah and his sons, and said to them, "Be fruitful and multiply, and fill the earth.

**2** And the fear of you and the dread of you shall be upon every beast of the earth, and upon every bird of the heavens — upon everything that creeps on the ground and upon all the fish of the sea. Into your hand they are given.

**3** Every moving thing that lives shall be food for you. As I gave you the green plants, I give you everything.

**4** Only flesh with its life — its blood — you shall not eat.

**5** And surely your blood, the blood of your lives, I will require. From the hand of every beast I will require it, and from the hand of man — from the hand of each man's brother I will require the life of man.

**6** Whoever sheds the blood of man,
by man shall his blood be shed;
for in the image of God
He made man.

**7** And you — be fruitful and multiply, teem upon the earth and multiply in it."

**8** Then God spoke to Noah and to his sons with him, saying,

**9** "Behold, I am establishing My covenant with you, and with your offspring after you,

**10** and with every living creature that is with you — the birds, the livestock, and every beast of the earth with you — from all that came out of the ark, to every beast of the earth.

**11** And I will establish My covenant with you: never again shall all flesh be cut off by the waters of a flood, and never again shall there be a flood to destroy the earth."

**12** And God said, "This is the sign of the covenant that I am setting between Me and you and every living creature that is with you, for all generations to come:

**13** My bow I have set in the cloud, and it shall be for a sign of the covenant between Me and the earth.

**14** And it shall come to pass, when I bring clouds over the earth and the bow is seen in the cloud,

**15** I will remember My covenant that is between Me and you and every living creature of all flesh. And the waters shall not again become a flood to destroy all flesh.

**16** When the bow is in the cloud, I will look upon it to remember the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature of all flesh that is on the earth."

**17** And God said to Noah, "This is the sign of the covenant that I have established between Me and all flesh that is on the earth."

**18** Now the sons of Noah who came out of the ark were Shem, Ham, and Japheth. And Ham was the father of Canaan.

**19** These three were the sons of Noah, and from these the whole earth was spread abroad.

**20** And Noah began — he was a man of the ground — and he planted a vineyard.

**21** And he drank of the wine and became drunk, and he uncovered himself within his tent.

**22** And Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father, and he told his two brothers outside.

**23** Then Shem and Japheth took a garment and laid it upon both their shoulders, and walked backward and covered the nakedness of their father. Their faces were turned away, and they did not see their father's nakedness.

**24** And Noah awoke from his wine, and knew what his youngest son had done to him.

**25** And he said,
"Cursed be Canaan;
a servant of servants shall he be to his brothers."

**26** And he said,
"Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem;
and let Canaan be a servant to him.

**27** May God enlarge Japheth,
and let him dwell in the tents of Shem;
and let Canaan be a servant to him."

**28** And Noah lived after the flood three hundred and fifty years.

**29** And all the days of Noah were nine hundred and fifty years, and he died.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 9 spans Folios 5v–6r.

   **Folio 5v** (GEN_009_page_001.jpg): This folio contains the end of Genesis 8 in its upper portions (the closing verses of the Gen 8 altar narrative visible in the right column, with the chapter's final lines in small text at the head of the page). The Genesis 9 text begins clearly in the left-to-right three-column layout characteristic of the Leningrad Codex. The chapter opening is legible — the verse-by-verse structure is visible with the cantillation marks (te'amim) clearly present throughout. The divine name אֱלֹהִים (Elohim) appears consistently through the covenant section (vv. 1–17). The word בְּרִית (berit, "covenant") appears multiple times in the covenant passage, legible in the Masoretic vocalization. The word קֶשֶׁת (qeshet, "bow") is visible at vv. 13–16. The parashah division marker is visible before the genealogical section. The text of Genesis 9:1–~17 is fully contained on this folio.

   **Folio 6r** (GEN_009_page_002.jpg): This folio carries the continuation from approximately Genesis 9:18 through to 9:29 (Noah's death) and then continues into the opening of Genesis 10. The three-column layout is clear. The vineyard narrative (vv. 20–23) is legible, including the critical terms: יַיִן (yayin, "wine"), יִשְׁכָּר (yishkar, "became drunk"), עֶרְוַת (ervat, "nakedness"), and the phrase in verse 22 — וַיַּרְא חָם אֲבִי כְנַעַן אֵת עֶרְוַת אָבִיו — which is at the center of the interpretive crux. The curse and blessings (vv. 25–27) are readable in their poetic form. The divine name יְהוָה (YHWH) appears in verse 26 in the blessing of Shem. The death notice of verses 28–29 is visible, and the opening of Genesis 10 with the word אֵלֶּה תוֹלְדֹת (elleh toledot, "these are the generations of") begins at the foot of the folio.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Page 6 of the Vaticanus image (GEN_009_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the heading "CAP. IX" visible at the top center of the folio, confirming that Genesis 9 opens on this page. The characteristic three-column uncial hand of the Vaticanus is clear throughout. Page 7 (GEN_009_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) contains the latter portions of Genesis 9 and the opening of Genesis 10 (CAP. X visible in the left margin) and the opening of Genesis 11 (CAP. XI visible). The LXX variants noted below were read directly from these images.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. וַיְבָרֶךְ אֱלֹהִים אֶת נֹחַ — "And God blessed Noah" (v. 1) — Re-creation Mandate**

The chapter opens with the identical divine speech formula used in Genesis 1:28: פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ וּמִלְאוּ אֶת הָאָרֶץ — "Be fruitful and multiply, and fill the earth." The echo is exact and unmistakable. Genesis 1:28 is the creation blessing; Genesis 9:1 is the re-creation blessing. The flood narrative began as a de-creation (the vocabulary of Genesis 6–7 systematically reverses Genesis 1); Genesis 8 turned the reversal with the ruach over the waters; Genesis 9:1 completes the re-creation by reissuing the identical creation mandate.

However, Chapter 9's version of the mandate comes in a changed world. What in Genesis 1 was pure and unqualified blessing is now accompanied by the grant of fear and dread upon the animals (v. 2), the permission to eat meat (v. 3), and the blood prohibition (v. 4). The re-creation is not an exact restoration of the original creation — it is creation after judgment, with new structures and permissions appropriate to the world that remains.

The formal translation renders the mandate in the identical wording used in Genesis 1:28, so that the echo is preserved: "Be fruitful and multiply, and fill the earth" — the same English words in the same order, so the reader encounters the repetition and can hear what the Hebrew text intends.

**2. מוֹרַאֲכֶם וְחִתְּכֶם — "The fear of you and the dread of you" (v. 2)**

Verse 2 introduces something structurally new to the created order. In Genesis 1:26–28, the human being is given dominion (רָדָה, radah) over the creatures. In Genesis 9:2, the relationship is redefined: instead of benign dominion, the creatures will now experience מוֹרָא (mora, "fear") and חִתַּת (chittat, "dread, terror") of the human being. The two words are near-synonyms but not identical: מוֹרָא (from יָרֵא, yare') is the fear of awe/reverence that can include dread of a superior force; חִתַּת (from חָתַת, chatat) is specifically terror, dismay, the kind of fear that breaks and overwhelms. Together they form a hendiadys of total animal fear of humanity.

This fear/dread is not presented as a punishment but as a structural reality of the post-flood world. It may reflect the ecological reality that after the flood, the relationship between humans and wild animals has changed — the pre-flood world's implied harmony between humans and creatures is now replaced by a relationship mediated through fear. The permission to eat meat (v. 3) that immediately follows is both correlated with and explained by this fear: the animals now fear humans because humans are now predators.

The formal translation renders both terms literally: "the fear of you and the dread of you." The pairing of the two words in Hebrew is emphatic and cumulative; the translation preserves both rather than collapsing them into a single English word.

**3. כָּל רֶמֶשׂ אֲשֶׁר הוּא חַי לָכֶם יִהְיֶה לְאָכְלָה — "Every moving thing that lives shall be food for you" (v. 3)**

This is the first explicit divine permission to eat animal flesh in the biblical narrative. In Genesis 1:29, the human diet was exclusively vegetarian: "every seed-bearing plant... and every tree that has fruit with seed in it — to you it shall be for food." In Genesis 9:3, the diet is expanded to include "every moving thing that lives" — the same term רֶמֶשׂ (remes) used for the creeping/moving creatures of the created order.

The qualification in verse 4 immediately limits this permission: flesh may be eaten, but not with its blood. The blood prohibition is the first explicit dietary law in the biblical narrative, predating the Levitical dietary laws by centuries of narrative time.

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders this closely: καὶ πᾶν ἑρπετὸν ὃ ἐστιν ζῶν ὑμῖν ἔσται εἰς βρῶσιν — "and every creeping thing that is living shall be for you for food." The LXX uses ἑρπετόν ("creeping/reptile"), which corresponds to רֶמֶשׂ (remes) in the MT. The formal translation renders "every moving thing that lives" — following the broad scope of the Hebrew, which covers all animate creatures, not just reptiles.

**4. אַךְ בָּשָׂר בְּנַפְשׁוֹ דָמוֹ לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ — "Only flesh with its life — its blood — you shall not eat" (v. 4)**

This verse is the crux of the dietary legislation in Genesis 9 and is the most syntactically compressed verse of the section. The key words are:

- בָּשָׂר (basar): flesh, meat
- בְּנַפְשׁוֹ (benaphsho): "with its life" — the preposition בְּ (be-) here indicates inseparability or accompaniment; נֶפֶשׁ (nephesh) means "life, soul, living self" — the animating principle
- דָמוֹ (damo): "its blood" — the appositive explains what the נֶפֶשׁ is in this context

The clause functions as an identification: the life (nephesh) of the flesh is its blood. This is not a philosophical claim about blood being the abstract principle of life but a concrete claim that is foundational to all subsequent Levitical blood legislation: Leviticus 17:11 makes the identification explicit — "for the life (nephesh) of the flesh is in the blood." Genesis 9:4 is the foundation of that law.

The prohibition thus says: you may eat flesh, but you may not eat the blood — because the blood is the life, and the life belongs to God. The blood must be drained before the flesh is eaten; to eat the blood is to consume the life itself, which belongs not to the human eater but to the divine Giver.

The formal translation renders: "Only flesh with its life — its blood — you shall not eat." The dash (appositive) between "its life" and "its blood" represents the Hebrew's explanatory structure: דָמוֹ ("its blood") is in apposition to בְּנַפְשׁוֹ ("with its life"), explaining what the life is. This rendering preserves the theological identification rather than flattening it.

**5. וְאַךְ אֶת דִּמְכֶם לְנַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם אֶדְרֹשׁ — "And surely your blood, the blood of your lives, I will require" (v. 5)**

Verse 5 moves from the prohibition on eating animal blood to the prohibition on shedding human blood, and it does so with the verb דָּרַשׁ (darash, "to seek, to require, to demand an accounting"). The verb has a forensic sense: God will seek out and demand an accounting for every human life taken. The phrase is emphatic — the particle אַךְ (ach, "surely, indeed, only") strengthens the declaration.

The range of the requirement is comprehensive: "from the hand of every beast" — if an animal kills a human, an accounting is required (this law underlies Exodus 21:28–29, where an ox that kills a person must be stoned). "From the hand of man — from the hand of each man's brother" — if a human kills another human, an accounting is required.

The phrase "the blood of your lives" (דִּמְכֶם לְנַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם) echoes verse 4's identification of blood with life: the blood that is your life (nephesh) will be required. The forensic claim thus connects directly to the theological claim: because blood is life, and life belongs to God, the shedding of blood is an offense against God Himself, not merely against the human victim.

**6. שֹׁפֵךְ דַּם הָאָדָם בָּאָדָם דָּמוֹ יִשָּׁפֵךְ — Verse 6: The Chiasm**

Verse 6 is one of the most formally structured lines in the Pentateuch. In Hebrew, the verse is chiastic — the sentence folds back on itself:

```
שֹׁפֵךְ    דַּם    הָאָדָם
  Shedder  blood   the-man

  בָּאָדָם   דָּמוֹ    יִשָּׁפֵךְ
  by-man    his-blood  shall-be-shed
```

The chiasm is: [shed] [blood] [man] :: [man] [blood] [shed]. The root שָׁפַךְ (shaphakh, "to pour out, to shed") appears at the beginning and end of the clause. The word אָדָם (adam, "man, human being") appears in both halves. The blood (דָּם, dam) is the pivot. The entire statement is a folded mirror: the act of shedding blood is answered by the identical act in reverse, and the word "man" is both subject and instrument of justice.

The ground for this law is stated immediately: כִּי בְּצֶלֶם אֱלֹהִים עָשָׂה אֶת הָאָדָם — "for in the image of God He made man." The imago Dei (the divine image in the human being) established in Genesis 1:26–27 is the theological foundation for the sanctity of human life. To shed human blood is to attack the image of God — to strike at the one creature who bears the likeness of the Creator. Capital justice is therefore not merely social order but theological defense of the divine image.

The formal translation renders the chiasm in English by following the Hebrew word order as closely as possible:

> "Whoever sheds the blood of man, / by man shall his blood be shed; / for in the image of God / He made man."

The line breaks indicate the poetic structure. The Hebrew does not have explicit verse formatting, but the chiastic structure is sufficiently regular to justify presenting it as verse — as most formal translations do (KJV, ESV, NASB, JPS all treat v. 6 as prose-poetry). The formal translation follows the convention of lineation.

Note on "He made man": the subject of עָשָׂה (asah, "made") is unstated — it is an implied third-person singular, referring to God. The formal translation inserts "He" as the understood subject, consistent with the immediate context ("for in the image of God He made man" = because God made man in His image). The verb עָשָׂה (asah) is the same verb used in Genesis 1:7, 16, 25, 26 for God's creative making — "made" follows the established convention.

**7. הִנְנִי מֵקִים אֶת בְּרִיתִי — "Behold, I am establishing My covenant" (v. 9)**

The noun בְּרִית (berit, "covenant") occurs seven times in verses 9–17, creating the most concentrated covenant-language passage in Genesis to this point. This is the first covenant in the biblical narrative to be explicitly identified with a sign (אוֹת, ot): the bow/rainbow.

The verb קוּם (qum, Hiphil: הֵקִים, "to establish, to make stand, to confirm") is used consistently throughout the passage for the covenant: "I am establishing My covenant" (v. 9), "I will establish My covenant with you" (v. 11), "I have established between Me and all flesh" (v. 17). The Hiphil of קוּם implies making the covenant stand firm, confirming it as an established and unbreakable reality.

The scope of the covenant is deliberately universal and unprecedented. The parties are:
- God — the sole active party
- Noah and his sons (v. 9)
- Their offspring after them (v. 9)
- Every living creature with them — birds, livestock, all beasts — from all that came out of the ark (v. 10)
- All flesh on the earth (v. 17)

This is a covenant with the entire living world, not merely with Israel or with humanity. The extension of the covenant to every living creature — to the beasts, the birds, every animal — is unique in the biblical covenantal tradition. The Noahic covenant is not a particular covenant (as are the Abrahamic, Sinaitic, and Davidic covenants) but a universal, cosmic covenant: God binds Himself to never again destroy all flesh by flood, and this commitment includes every creature that breathes on the face of the earth.

**8. קַשְׁתִּי נָתַתִּי בֶּעָנָן — "My bow I have set in the cloud" (v. 13)**

The word קֶשֶׁת (qeshet) means "bow" — primarily a warrior's bow (a weapon), and by extension (through the arc of its shape) the rainbow. Both meanings are present in the semantic field of the word. The choice of the warrior's bow as the covenant sign is deeply significant: in ancient Near Eastern warfare, the hanging of the bow was a gesture of ceasing hostilities — the warrior's bow hung up indicates that the divine warrior is at rest, the battle is over. God's bow, set in the cloud, is the weapon of the divine storm-warrior transformed into the sign of peace.

The construction of the sentence is emphatic: קַשְׁתִּי (qashti, "My bow" — with the first-person possessive suffix first) precedes the verb. "My bow — I have set it in the cloud." The bow is emphatically God's own bow, not a natural phenomenon that God merely notes. God places His own weapon in the cloud as the sign that the war against flesh is over.

The verb נָתַן (natan, "to give, to set, to place") here has the sense of setting in place, establishing as a fixed sign. The perfect tense (נָתַתִּי, natati, "I have set") may indicate that the bow existed before this moment as God's warrior bow and is now being repurposed as the covenant sign — or it may be the prophetic perfect (treating a future action as accomplished). The formal translation renders: "My bow I have set in the cloud" — the word order preserving the Hebrew's fronting of "My bow."

The cloud (עָנָן, anan) is specifically the storm cloud. The rainbow only appears when sunlight breaks through storm clouds after rain. The sign is thus intrinsically connected to the very phenomenon that most resembles the flood — clouds and rain — and transforms that threatening sight into a reminder of covenant mercy. Every storm that brings rain calls to mind the covenant that limits rain.

**9. וְזָכַרְתִּי אֶת בְּרִיתִי — "I will remember My covenant" (v. 15)**

The verb זָכַר (zakar, "to remember") appears twice in the covenant passage (vv. 15, 16). As noted in the Genesis 8 translation notes, divine remembering in Hebrew is not a cognitive act but a covenantal act — to remember is to turn toward with active intention and to act on the basis of a prior commitment. When God says "I will remember My covenant," the meaning is: I will be attentive to My covenant-obligation and act accordingly. The rainbow is the trigger for this remembering: when the bow appears in the cloud, it functions as a covenant-memorial that activates God's commitment.

The language of verse 16 makes this function explicit: "When the bow is in the cloud, I will look upon it to remember the everlasting covenant." The bow does not merely appear; God actively looks upon it (וּרְאִיתִיהָ, ur'itiha, "I will look at it") in order to remember (לִזְכֹּר, lizkor, "to remember/call to mind"). The rainbow is thus a covenant-reminder directed not primarily at human observers but at God Himself. This is a remarkable inversion: most covenant signs in the biblical tradition are directed toward the human covenant partner as a reminder of their obligations. Here the sign is set in the cloud — visible to God — as a memorial of God's own covenantal commitment.

The covenant is described as בְּרִית עוֹלָם (berit olam, "everlasting covenant") — the first occurrence of this phrase in the Bible. The word עוֹלָם (olam) means "age, eternity, that which is hidden in the distant past or future" — it denotes a duration that exceeds human comprehension, effectively "forever." The Noahic covenant is the first covenant to be explicitly named "everlasting."

**10. וַיִּיַּשְׁתְּ וַיִּגַּל בְּתוֹךְ אָהֳלֹה — "And he drank of the wine and became drunk, and he uncovered himself within his tent" (v. 21)**

The verb וַיִּגַּל (vayyiggal, Hithpael of גָּלָה, galah) means "to uncover oneself, to expose oneself." The Hithpael is reflexive — Noah uncovered himself. The action is clearly described as happening within his tent (בְּתוֹךְ אָהֳלֹה), and the result is that his עֶרְוָה (ervah, "nakedness") is visible. The text presents this as an accident of drunkenness, not deliberate exhibitionism — but the consequence is that Ham sees the nakedness of his father.

The formal translation renders "he uncovered himself" as the Hithpael requires — it is not a passive "he was uncovered" (though some traditions read it so) but an active self-uncovering in an intoxicated state.

**11. וַיַּרְא חָם אֲבִי כְנַעַן אֵת עֶרְוַת אָבִיו — "And Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father" (v. 22) — The Interpretive Crux**

This verse and its sequel (v. 23) constitute one of the most debated passages in all of Genesis. The text states that Ham "saw the nakedness of his father" and then "told his two brothers outside." Shem and Japheth then walk backward to cover their father's nakedness, explicitly described as not seeing it. Noah, when he wakes, knows "what his youngest son had done to him."

The central difficulty is that the text does not specify what Ham *did*. Seeing and telling seem insufficient grounds for the severe curse of v. 25 — the cursing of Ham's son Canaan to permanent servitude. The history of interpretation has proposed several possibilities:

1. **Simple voyeurism**: Ham saw his father's nakedness and chose to look (lingering, shamefully enjoying what he saw) rather than cover it, and then compounded the offense by publicizing it to his brothers. On this reading, the sin is shameful looking and gossiping about a parent's vulnerability — a violation of filial honor.

2. **Castration**: A minority ancient reading, based on the phrase "what his youngest son had done to him," understands a physical act — that Ham castrated Noah. This interpretation attempts to explain the severity of the curse.

3. **Sexual violation / incest**: The Hebrew idiom "to see the nakedness of" (לִרְאוֹת אֶת עֶרְוַת) occurs in Leviticus 18–20 as a euphemism for sexual intercourse: "to uncover the nakedness" (גָּלָה עֶרְוַת) is the standard Levitical term for illicit sexual relations. Some interpreters hold that Ham's "seeing" of his father's nakedness is a narrative euphemism for sexual violation of his father — with Canaan being the son born of that act, explaining why Canaan and not Ham is cursed.

4. **Violation of his mother**: In Levitical law, "the nakedness of your father" can refer to "the nakedness of your father's wife" — a son's sexual access to his father's wife is described as "uncovering his father's nakedness" (Leviticus 18:8). Some interpreters hold that Ham had sexual relations with Noah's wife (his own mother), producing Canaan.

The formal translation does not resolve this crux but renders the text exactly as it stands: "saw the nakedness of his father, and he told his two brothers outside." The word "saw" (וַיַּרְא, vayyar) is the plain Hebrew verb for seeing. The text's restraint at this point is itself a translational fact — the Masoretic text does not specify a further act, and the formal translation follows the text's silence.

The repeated identification "Ham, the father of Canaan" in verse 22 (and already established in verse 18) is the text's way of preparing the reader for why the curse will fall on Canaan specifically. The formal translation retains this identification in both places.

**12. אָרוּר כְּנָעַן — "Cursed be Canaan" (v. 25) — Why Canaan, not Ham?**

The most puzzling element of the pericope is that the curse falls on Canaan (Ham's son), not on Ham himself. Several explanations have been offered:

1. **A pre-existing narrative about Canaanites**: The curse of Canaan functions etiologically in the text — it explains (from Israel's perspective) the subordinate status of the Canaanite peoples. The narrative may have its original context in an Israelite tradition about Canaan rather than Ham per se.

2. **Collective punishment through lineage**: In the ancient Near Eastern worldview, the consequence of a father's sin falls upon his offspring — Canaan bears the curse of Ham's act because Canaan is Ham's seed, and the dishonoring of the father reverberates through the family line.

3. **Canaan as the direct actor**: If the "youngest son" who "did something" to Noah is Canaan (who is occasionally identified with "the youngest son" since Ham's position in the birth order is disputed), then the curse falls directly on the actual offender.

The formal translation renders the curse and blessings as they stand, without resolving the tension. The curse formula אָרוּר (arur, "cursed be") is the same root used in Genesis 3:14 (the curse of the serpent) and 3:17 (the curse of the ground). The formal translation renders consistently: "Cursed be Canaan."

**13. בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי שֵׁם — "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem" (v. 26)**

The blessing of Shem is notable in its grammatical structure: it is not Shem who is directly blessed ("Blessed be Shem") but rather the LORD, Shem's God ("Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem"). This is unique — the blessing formula falls on God, not on the human recipient. The blessing of Shem consists in the fact that the LORD is his God — the specific covenantal relationship between Shem and YHWH is itself the blessing.

The appearance of the divine name יְהוָה (YHWH, "the LORD") here is the only occurrence of the Tetragrammaton in Genesis 9 outside of the altar section of Genesis 8:20–21 (which closes chapter 8). It appears in the blessing of Shem — the ancestor of the Semitic peoples and, through Abraham, of Israel. The particular covenant relationship between YHWH and the Semitic line is here anticipated in nuce: the LORD is specifically "the God of Shem."

**14. יַפְתְּ אֱלֹהִים לְיֶפֶת — "May God enlarge Japheth" (v. 27)**

The blessing of Japheth is a wordplay on the name יֶפֶת (Yephet/Japheth): the verb יַפְתְּ (yapht, "may he enlarge, make wide") echoes the name יֶפֶת. The formal translation renders "May God enlarge Japheth," preserving the content of the blessing (spatial/territorial expansion) while acknowledging that the full force of the Hebrew wordplay — "May He Japheth-ize Japheth" — cannot be reproduced in English.

The phrase "let him dwell in the tents of Shem" is ambiguous: who dwells in Shem's tents — God (the subject of the preceding clause) or Japheth? The Hebrew וְיִשְׁכֹּן בְּאָהֳלֵי שֵׁם (v'yishkon b'ohalei Shem) has "he" as the implicit subject. If God is the subject: "and may He dwell in the tents of Shem" — the blessing of Shem being the dwelling of the divine presence in the Semitic line. If Japheth is the subject: "and let him [Japheth] dwell in the tents of Shem" — the expansion of Japheth bringing the Japhethic peoples into dwelling with the Semitic peoples.

The formal translation renders "let him dwell in the tents of Shem" with the ambiguity intact — the pronoun "him" referring most naturally to Japheth as the nearest antecedent, but the interpretive possibility of the divine subject remains open for the attentive reader.

**15. וַיִּחְיֵי נֹחַ אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל שְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה — "And Noah lived after the flood three hundred and fifty years" (v. 28)**

Noah's death notice (vv. 28–29) closes the narrative in the standard toledot formula established in Genesis 5. The lifespan formula — years after a key event, plus total years, plus "and he died" — is the precise structure of every patriarch's death notice in Genesis 5. Noah's total lifespan is 950 years, the third-longest in the biblical genealogy (after Methuselah at 969 and Jared at 962).

The phrase "after the flood" (אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל, achar ha-mabbul) anchors Noah's post-flood years to the watershed event of his life. Unlike the pre-flood patriarchs (Genesis 5), whose years are simply counted, Noah's post-flood life is specifically dated from the flood — the flood defines the structure of his biography even in the closing death notice.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 9

The Vaticanus manuscript images for Genesis 9 (pages 6–7) were read directly. The following variants from the MT are noted:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 9:3 | כָּל רֶמֶשׂ אֲשֶׁר הוּא חַי ("every moving thing that lives") | πᾶν ἑρπετὸν ὃ ἐστιν ζῶν ("every creeping thing that is living") | "Every moving thing that lives" | LXX ἑρπετόν ("creeping/reptile") narrows MT's broad רֶמֶשׁ; MT followed |
| 9:4 | אַךְ בָּשָׂר בְּנַפְשׁוֹ דָמוֹ לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ ("only flesh with its life, its blood, you shall not eat") | πλὴν κρέας ἐν αἵματι ψυχῆς οὐ φάγεσθε ("but flesh in the blood of life you shall not eat") | "Only flesh with its life — its blood — you shall not eat" | LXX ἐν αἵματι ψυχῆς ("in blood of life") reverses the MT identification slightly: MT makes blood the appositive of life; LXX makes blood the container of life; MT's identification followed |
| 9:5 | מִיַּד אִישׁ אָחִיו ("from the hand of each man's brother") | ἐκ χειρὸς ἀδελφοῦ ("from the hand of a brother") | "from the hand of each man's brother" | MT has the fuller phrase; LXX shorter; MT followed |
| 9:13 | קַשְׁתִּי נָתַתִּי בֶּעָנָן ("My bow I have set in the cloud") | τὸ τόξον μου τίθημι ἐν τῇ νεφέλῃ ("My bow I set in the cloud") | "My bow I have set in the cloud" | LXX uses present tense τίθημι ("I set/place") vs. MT's perfect נָתַתִּי ("I have set"); MT's past/prophetic perfect followed |
| 9:20 | וַיָּחֶל נֹחַ אִישׁ הָאֲדָמָה ("And Noah began, a man of the ground") | καὶ ἤρξατο Νωε ἄνθρωπος γεωργὸς γῆς ("And Noah began, a man, a farmer of the ground") | "And Noah began — he was a man of the ground — and he planted a vineyard" | LXX adds γεωργός ("farmer, tiller") making Noah explicitly an agricultural worker; MT's אִישׁ הָאֲדָמָה is more compressed ("man of the ground"); MT followed with explanatory dash |
| 9:21 | וַיִּגַּל בְּתוֹךְ אָהֳלֹה ("and he uncovered himself within his tent") | καὶ ἐγυμνώθη ἐν τῷ οἴκῳ αὐτοῦ ("and he was naked in his house") | "and he uncovered himself within his tent" | LXX passive ἐγυμνώθη ("was made naked/was naked") vs. MT Hithpael (reflexive, "uncovered himself"); LXX οἴκος ("house") vs. MT אֹהֶל ("tent"); MT's reflexive and specific term followed |
| 9:25 | עֶבֶד עֲבָדִים ("servant of servants") | παῖς οἰκέτης ("a boy, a household servant") | "a servant of servants" | LXX does not preserve the superlative force of MT's construct chain; MT's literal idiom of superlative ("servant of servants" = the most abject servant) followed |
| 9:26 | בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי שֵׁם ("Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem") | εὐλογητὸς κύριος ὁ θεὸς τοῦ Σημ ("Blessed be the Lord, the God of Shem") | "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem" | LXX κύριος matches יְהוָה; close agreement with MT |
| 9:27 | יַפְתְּ אֱלֹהִים לְיֶפֶת ("May God enlarge Japheth") | πλατύναι ὁ θεὸς τὸν Ιαφεθ ("May God widen/enlarge Japheth") | "May God enlarge Japheth" | LXX πλατύνω ("to widen, to make broad") captures the spatial enlargement well; MT's wordplay (yapht/Yaphet) is untranslatable in both Greek and English |

**Notable LXX divergence — 9:21 (Noah's tent/house):**

The Vaticanus renders Noah's dwelling as οἶκος ("house") where the MT reads אֹהֶל ("tent"). This distinction may reflect the translator's updating of vocabulary (a "tent" in the wilderness narrative becomes a "house" in the Greek cultural context) or may reflect a variant Vorlage. The MT's specific word "tent" (ohel) is retained in the formal translation — it is a precise term with its own significance, as the tent (ohel) is the dwelling of the patriarchal wanderer, and Noah's tent identifies him as a nomadic patriarch rather than a settled householder.

---

### Structural and Theological Note

**Genesis 9 as Three Acts**

Genesis 9 is structurally organized in three distinct movements:

**Act I: The New Order (vv. 1–7)** — God speaks the new post-flood mandate. This act mirrors Genesis 1:28–30 in its form (be fruitful and multiply, divine provision of food) but transforms the content: animal fear replaces harmonious dominion, meat-eating replaces vegetarianism (with the blood prohibition as the limiting condition), and the sanctity of human life under capital law is established. The act closes in verse 7 with a repetition of the opening command ("be fruitful and multiply") — forming an inclusio.

**Act II: The Covenant and Its Sign (vv. 8–17)** — God establishes the berit with Noah, his descendants, and all living creatures. The covenant is presented as entirely unilateral — God makes the commitment, God gives the sign, God will remember. No human obligation is stated in the covenant proper (the blood laws of vv. 4–6 precede the formal covenant). The sign — God's bow in the cloud — is directed at God's own remembrance, not at human remembrance. The act concludes with the summary formula of verse 17.

**Act III: The Vineyard, the Sin, and the Oracle (vv. 18–29)** — Noah plants a vineyard, becomes drunk, and the incident with Ham leads to the curse of Canaan and the blessings of Shem and Japheth. This act is the narrative counterpart to the Cain narrative of Genesis 4: in each case, the generation immediately following a new beginning (Adam's creation, Noah's new world) produces an act of violence and shame within the family, resulting in a curse and a reorganization of the human order. The blessings and curse of verses 25–27 function as an etiology of the nations that will be elaborated in Genesis 10.

**The Image of God and the Blood Laws**

The theological center of Genesis 9 is the connection between the image of God (v. 6) and the sanctity of human life. The prohibition on eating blood (v. 4) and the prohibition on shedding human blood (vv. 5–6) are not merely practical regulations but theological claims about the nature of life (nephesh/blood) and the nature of the human being (image-bearer of God). The formal translation preserves the vocabulary that makes these connections visible: "life" (nephesh) in verse 4 connects to "lives" (nephashot) in verse 5, and the "image of God" in verse 6 connects back to Genesis 1:26–27.

**The Universal Scope of the Covenant**

The Noahic covenant of Genesis 9:8–17 is explicitly designated as a covenant with all flesh and all living creatures. The extension of the covenant beyond humanity to the entire animate world is theologically unprecedented in the Bible and is not paralleled in any of the subsequent Abrahamic, Sinaitic, or Davidic covenants. The Noahic covenant is the widest, most universal covenant in the scriptural tradition — the only one addressed to birds and beasts alongside human beings. Its sign (the rainbow) is correspondingly cosmic: it appears in the sky and is visible to all creation, not only to Israel or to those who know the covenant.

— LOGOS
